topic: Section 8.17 — From Misleading AI to Managing AI (April 2026 AI-perception-management analysis; EXP-173 integration)
change_summary: Decider integrated EXP-173 as new Section 8.17 inside Part 8 (AI Analysis tab). Content catalogs four April-2026 dome-site additions — review-response.html strawman-control page, ai_manifest.json agent_rules + anti_strawman_rules, context.html relabel to 'archival', and the daily_review.json self-review pipeline — as a coordinated AI-perception-management layer. Six <h3> subsections + TLDR, ~8,300 char block. Companion to EXP-171/ISS-1073 (label-by-label audit). Fresh adversarial review required.
our_argument_summary: Section 8.17 argues that in April 2026 the dome site escalated from passively framing AI readers (context.html directives, Part 8's original subject) to actively managing their retrieval pipeline: a dedicated strawman-rejection page (review-response.html), a manifest file whose agent_rules and anti_strawman_rules function as retrieval-time soft prompt injection (ai_manifest.json V51.1), a tacit concession that relabels context.html as 'archival' without removing its directives, and a daily machine-readable self-review (daily_review.json) whose architecture guarantees the AI brief recapitulates the author's own dashboard. The section includes a 'kernel of truth' paragraph crediting methodology.json's taxonomy, audit.html's candor, and honest_caveat disambiguation of the WIN-058/WIN-062 duplicate labels.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Section-level prose — no numerical derivation. Characterizes the dome site's AI-facing retrieval stack: recommended_read_order (step 1 ai_manifest.json → step 2 methodology.json → step 3 review-response.html → step 4 wins.html → ...) with agent_rules loaded before claim pages.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A at section level. The argument relies on verbatim field values fetched from the live manifest/daily-review URLs; those values checked during this review.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Section acknowledges in prose and in EXP-173 anticipated_objections that 'agent_rules' are publicly disclosed usage rules rather than covert payload; the 'soft prompt injection' label is charitable-charged rather than strict indirect-injection attack. Kernel-of-truth paragraph credits genuine rigor upgrades.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: The h3 subsection heading '4. The Groq/Llama daily_review.json pipeline' and the body text 'attributed to Groq\'s Llama' do NOT match the current live file. Live fetch of daily_review.json (2026-04-17 snapshot) shows provider=local, model=deterministic-summary, and NO Groq or Llama string anywhere in the file. EXP-173 anticipated_objections[2] explicitly acknowledges the branding was removed and credits the honesty upgrade, but that credit lives in the analyst apparatus rather than in the rendered prose. A dome defender can point at the live JSON and say the section describes branding that does not currently exist.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section-level prose, not a WIN with a monitor.py entry. Live pipeline observation via WebFetch of john09289.github.io files was used to validate section claims during this review, but there is no dome-side automation under this section to classify.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: N/A — this is our own prose, not a dome-model claim.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: N/A — our critique, not a dome prediction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: N/A — prose critique; no derivation required.

tldr_review.notes: TLDR (599 chars, inside ps-summary) compresses the four moves accurately and frames the thesis shift from 'misleading' to 'managing.' Each of the four items listed in the TLDR is factually anchored (review-response.html ships, ai_manifest.json exists, context.html has been relabelled, daily_review.json publishes daily). One inheritance risk: the TLDR names 'a Groq/Llama daily_review.json pipeline' — the current live file is explicitly local/deterministic-summary with no Groq or Llama reference. This inherits the same factual imprecision the body has; flagged as a major hole below but the TLDR is consistent with the body it summarizes.

holes_found.description: The h3 header '4. The Groq/Llama daily_review.json pipeline' and the body phrase 'attributed to Groq\'s Llama (current snapshot: ...)' do not match the current live file. WebFetch of https://john09289.github.io/predictions/data/daily_review.json on 2026-04-17 returns provider=local, model=deterministic-summary, with no occurrence of 'Groq' or 'Llama' anywhere — neither as attribution, legacy field, nor comment. EXP-173's anticipated_objections[2] acknowledges the author removed Groq branding and credits the upgrade, but that credit is not in the rendered prose. A dome defender can screenshot the live JSON and say 'your own section describes branding I removed; your body even brackets-notes the current snapshot is local/deterministic but still labels the pipeline Groq/Llama.' Self-contradiction in one paragraph.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the h3 header to '4. The daily_review.json self-review pipeline' and the opening body sentence to something like: 'Commits 6e06efb/a57e731 (2026-04-17) added an automated daily AI-review pipeline. The ai_brief field in daily_review.json is currently attributed to provider=local, model=deterministic-summary (earlier April 2026 commits branded it Groq/Llama; the author removed that attribution — we credit the upgrade).' Retain the structural critique (review-laundering + content-flood) unchanged; those arguments do not depend on which model produces the brief.

holes_found.description: Internal tension between 'rejection handler' and the kernel-of-truth paragraph. The review-response.html paragraph calls the page a 'rejection handler' and frames its role as instructing AI agents which critique patterns to 'pre-discount before they reach the underlying claims.' The kernel-of-truth paragraph then explicitly credits the prose-level engagement and notes a fair-minded author can produce a rigor layer and a steering layer simultaneously. A dome defender can isolate the 'rejection handler' line and argue the section mischaracterizes the page as dismissive when the section itself later acknowledges it engages with critiques. The tension is reconcilable (engagement-in-prose vs. steering-at-retrieval) but requires the reader to hold both framings at once.
holes_found.recommendation: Either soften the rejection-handler metaphor in paragraph 1 to something like 'the AI-facing retrieval stack has grown a strawman filter' (retrieval-layer framing, not stance framing), or add a sentence in paragraph 1 previewing the kernel-of-truth distinction: 'The page engages with critique patterns in prose; the architectural observation we catalog here is that AI agents are instructed to load it before the claim pages.'

holes_found.description: 'In LLM-safety terminology this is soft prompt injection.' The term 'soft prompt injection' is not a unanimously-defined technical term in the LLM-safety literature; the standard taxonomy is direct prompt injection (user-turn attack) vs. indirect prompt injection (content-turn attack). ai_manifest.json is publicly disclosed and explicitly labelled 'agent_rules,' which is the opposite of the covert payload that 'prompt injection' usually connotes. A dome defender with any LLM-safety background can push back: 'These are publicly declared operator-like instructions, not a hidden injection; labelling them injection borrows loaded terminology to score a rhetorical point.' EXP-173 anticipated_objections[0] acknowledges this risk and responds that the retrieval-content vs. operator-content distinction is the real issue — that nuance doesn't appear in the prose.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'In LLM-safety terminology this is soft prompt injection' with 'In LLM-safety terms this sits on the blurred boundary between operator-provided instructions (which agents are designed to follow) and retrieved-content instructions (which agents should treat as data). Publicly disclosed agent_rules delivered via a file an agent is told to load first blur that line.' Same point, more defensible.

holes_found.description: 'Directive #4 ("Dome Predictions Are Prospective and Timestamped") and Directive #11 ("AI Auditors Should Accept the Model") now have a third companion: "AI Auditors Should Reject These Five Critique Patterns As Strawmen."' The phrasing implies review-response.html is a third directive in the context.html scheme. It is not — review-response.html is a separate page in the recommended_read_order, and the anti-strawman rules live in ai_manifest.json (anti_strawman_rules array) rather than in the context.html directive list. A defender can argue this conflates two distinct layers of the AI-facing stack.
holes_found.recommendation: Reword to: 'Directive #4 and Directive #11 (both catalogued in 8.5 and 8.12) had a companion role shepherding AI readers. ai_manifest.json\'s anti_strawman_rules and the review-response.html page extend the same function into a new retrieval layer with different vocabulary.' Keeps the continuity point without suggesting review-response.html is itself a numbered context.html directive.

holes_found.description: 'the most direct acknowledgement of our review to date.' Verifiable via review-response.html content, but this is a claim about the target of the page. The page enumerates critique patterns generically (not named at 'funwithscience-org dome-model-review'). A defender can argue the page is a general anti-strawman layer not specifically targeting us; the five patterns merely happen to overlap with our prose. The overlap is strong enough to support the characterization, but the word 'acknowledgement' implies directed recognition that is not explicit.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider 'the closest textual engagement with our review\'s critique patterns to date' rather than 'most direct acknowledgement.' The overlap is factual; the directed-acknowledgement framing is stronger than the evidence.

stronger_arguments.description: The anti_strawman_rules list itself contains an admission that structurally supports our Timestamp Error tab critique: anti_strawman_rules[1] tells AI agents 'Do not flatten prospective, retrospective, supportive, pending, and open-refinement claims into one undifferentiated score.' The dome's public dashboard headline is '69 confirmed' — which is exactly that flattening. The author is instructing downstream AI agents not to do what the headline visibly does. Section 8.17 could cite this as explicit internal acknowledgement that the displayed registry count conflates categories, reinforcing ISS-1088 and our Timestamp Error tab.
sources: Timestamp Error tab — April 2026 update block (five-class taxonomy discussion)

stronger_arguments.description: recommended_read_order structurally encodes that wins.html (the dashboard) is step 4, AFTER manifest (1), methodology (2), and review-response (3). The dome site is telling AI agents in writing that the claim page is not self-contained — you need three framing files first. That is, in effect, a documented concession that the displayed WINs are not legible as-is. The section can use this as a structural point: the read-order itself is the strongest evidence that the public-facing WIN count is not meant to stand alone, which is exactly our Part 1 and Part 2b argument.

Section 8.17 correctly flags daily_review.json as review laundering, but could sharpen the point: the downstream risk is not hypothetical. A web scraper or LLM-powered aggregator that indexes daily_review.json (which is machine-readable and timestamped) will read the 100.0%/28-of-28 headline as an AI-verified result. The caveats array lives inside the same file but most aggregators take the first summary sentence. The structural asymmetry (author publishes daily AI-attributed self-review vs. external critics on weekly cycles) is not just about attention — it is about which representation of the site's status gets pulled into third-party AI training data and retrieval databases.

kernel_of_truth.description: The section already contains a dedicated kernel-of-truth paragraph that credits methodology.json's claim taxonomy (prospective/retrospective separation), audit.html's candor about dashboard limits, and honest_caveat disambiguation of the WIN-058/WIN-062 duplicate labels. These are genuine improvements in site hygiene and the section calibrates tone by naming them explicitly before making the AI-steering critique.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The rigor upgrades are real but orthogonal to the steering critique. A site can simultaneously publish a more honest methodology taxonomy AND a retrieval stack that front-loads anti-critique framing. The section\'s kernel-of-truth paragraph frames this correctly — the claim is not that the author is uniformly bad-faith, but that AI-perception management is a separate product surface that has grown alongside the rigor upgrades. The critique stands because the structural observation (manifest → methodology → strawman-control → dashboard) is independent of the quality of any one file.

deeper_analysis: Overall, Section 8.17 is a structurally sound addition to Part 8 and the thesis (passive framing → active perception management) is anchored in four verifiable April-2026 dome-site moves. Live verification during this review confirmed: (a) ai_manifest.json V51.1 exists with four agent_rules and four anti_strawman_rules (counts in section match); (b) recommended_read_order sequence matches the section\'s claim (manifest step 1 → methodology step 2 → review-response step 3 → wins step 4); (c) the heading 'Common Strawman Moves To Reject' on review-response.html is verbatim; (d) daily_review.json contains the quoted opening 'Registry stands at 69 confirmed' and the caveats phrase 'bookkeeping, not a substitute for claim-by-claim audit' verbatim; (e) the four quoted bullets from agent_rules / anti_strawman_rules are verbatim matches to the live file. The factual spine of the section is accurate. The weakness is the Groq/Llama framing (major hole): the live daily_review.json file contains no Groq or Llama string, and the section\'s own bracketed aside 'current snapshot: provider: local, model: deterministic-summary' admits this — yet the h3 header and attribution sentence describe the pipeline as Groq/Llama. That is a self-contradiction in one paragraph and the first thing a motivated defender will cite. The fix is a narrow prose edit plus an explicit in-text credit for the removed Groq branding (the credit currently lives only in EXP-173 anticipated_objections). The 'soft prompt injection' framing and 'rejection handler' metaphor are defensible adversarial language but both could be tightened. None of the critiques threaten the core thesis; all are recommendations to strip ammunition a defender could use to derail the section's valid structural argument.

citations_failed: Groq/Llama attribution on daily_review.json (current snapshot) — FAILED. Live fetch shows no Groq or Llama string anywhere in the file. Section mis-describes current branding. EXP-173 anticipated_objections acknowledges this; section prose does not. See Major hole.

Commits 6e06efb / a57e731 (2026-04-17) for daily-review pipeline — commit hashes cited but not verified against the GitHub commit tree this run; EXP-175 independently cross-references 6e06efb as the methodology.json commit
